highlights: Aragon is developing enterprise "company world models" that utilize reinforcement learning to update model weights based on internal data like Slack and emails. This approach allows businesses to "own their intelligence" while significantly reducing costs and increasing performance compared to closed-source frontier models.

Drone logistics is shifting toward FAA Part 108 regulations, which allow for autonomous flights beyond visual line of sight and fleet management by remote operators. New hybrid tilt-rotor drones can now carry 44-pound payloads over 60 miles, creating a "physical internet" to service remote and rural areas.

AI-generated backgrounds and lighting are drastically reducing film production costs, allowing high-quality features to be shot for $70 million instead of $200 million. This technology enables actors to perform on simplified soundstages while AI handles complex environmental rendering, shifting the focus back toward creative performance.

Google co-founder Sergey Brin has directed Gemini engineers to use internal AI agents for complex, multi-step tasks to accelerate development through "dogfooding." This reflects a broader industry trend toward recursive AI development, where models are used to build and refine more advanced versions of themselves.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* centers on the shift toward specialized, autonomous intelligence in both the digital and physical worlds. The first guest, Josh Sarody, CEO of Aragon, discusses the emergence of "company world models." These allow enterprises to "own their intelligence" by training small language models (SLMs) on proprietary data using reinforcement learning. Unlike massive public models, these SLMs are more cost-effective and faster because the company’s specific knowledge is baked into the model weights rather than processed through expensive, large context windows. The hosts highlight a broader industry trend where businesses are moving away from general-purpose AI toward custom, agentic systems that can autonomously handle internal tasks like recruitment, email management, and even financial transactions.

In the physical realm, the conversation shifts to autonomous logistics with Etienne Louvet, CEO of Iona. Louvet showcases a "light cargo" drone system designed to create a "physical internet" for rural and remote areas, bypassing traditional road infrastructure. A key point of the discussion is the regulatory evolution of the FAA's Part 108, which facilitates "Beyond Visual Line of Sight" (BVLOS) operations. This framework allows drones to be managed like air traffic rather than individual piloted aircraft, enabling high-density delivery fleets. By utilizing tilt-rotor hybrid technology, Iona’s drones can carry up to 44 pounds over long distances, positioning them as a more efficient alternative to traditional delivery vans for middle-mile logistics.

The episode also covers broader AI trends, including Moonshot AI’s latest open-source releases and the "dogfooding" of AI agents within major tech firms like Google. A recurring theme is the increasing power of local computing; the hosts envision a future where high-performance agents run relentlessly on personal workstations rather than in the cloud. The show concludes with a look at AI’s impact on Hollywood, specifically a new film starring Gal Gadot that uses AI-generated backgrounds to drastically reduce production costs. Finally, the hosts touch on lifestyle and health, discussing the benefits of high-protein "tinned fish" diets for founders looking to maintain muscle mass while utilizing modern weight-loss medications.
